narration: Create a folder named server code and open it in VS Code.
Create a file named server.js.
Import the http module using the require function.
Define a constant variable named http.
Use the http.createServer function to create a server.
Define a callback function that accepts request and response parameters.
Use the response.end function to send a response back to the client.
Set the response body to 'hello world'.
Use the server.listen function to start the server on port 3000.
Add a callback function to the listen method to log a message to the console.
Run the server using the node command followed by the file name.
Access the server using a browser request to localhost:3000.
Verify the response is 'hello world' in the browser.
Check the network tab to confirm the 200 OK response.
Attempt to send JSON data by setting the Content-Type header to application/json.
Use the response.setHeader function to set the Content-Type header.
Send the JSON data using the response.end function.
Restart the server to test the changes.
Observe that the Content-Type header is missing in the network tab.
Identify the error: 'Cannot set headers after they are sent to the client'.
Refactor the code to set the header before calling response.end.
Create a JavaScript object containing name, age, and channel.
Attempt to send the JavaScript object directly as the response body.
Observe the error: 'chunk argument must be of type string or an instance of buffer'.
Convert the JavaScript object to a string using the JSON.stringify function.
Send the stringified JSON data using the response.end function.
Verify the JSON data is correctly sent in the network tab.
Explain that the current server is static and does not verify routes.
Explain that the server does not serve content based on the requested route.
Explain that routing will be covered in the next video.
Explain the createServer function and its request and response parameters.
Explain the listen function and its callback parameter.
Summarize the functions used in the video.
